test_text.title: Renting a Cottage: Details and Arrangements

audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man phoning the owner of a holiday cottage.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello?
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Oh hello.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I was hoping to speak to Jack Fitzgerald about renting a cottage.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I'm his wife Shirley, and we own the cottages together, so I'm sure I can help you.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Great, my name's Tom.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Some friends of ours rented Granary Cottage from you last year, and they thought it was great.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: So my wife and I are hoping to come in May for a week.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: What date did you have in mind?
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: The week beginning the 14th, if possible.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I'll just check.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: I'm sorry, Tom.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It's already booked that week.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It's free the week beginning the 28th though, for seven nights.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: In fact, that's the only time you could have it in May.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Oh, well, we could manage that, I think.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: We'd just need to change a couple of things.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: How much would it cost?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That's the beginning of high season, so it'd be £550 for the week.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Ah, that's a bit more than we wanted to pay, I'm afraid.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: We've budgeted up to £500 for accommodation.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Well, we've just finished converting another building into a cottage,
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: which we're calling Chervil Cottage.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Sorry? what was that again?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Chervil. C H E R, V for Victor, I L.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Oh, that's a herb, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It grows fairly wild around here.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: You could have that for the week you want for £480.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: OK, so could you tell me something about it, please?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: The building was built as a garage.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It's a little smaller than Granary Cottage.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: So that must sleep two people as well.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: There's a double bedroom.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Does it have a garden?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, you get to it from the living room through French doors.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: And we provide two deck chairs.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: We hope to build a patio in the near future, but I wouldn't like to guarantee it'll be finished by May.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: The front door opens onto the old farmyard, and parking isn't a problem.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: There's plenty of room at the front for that.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: There are some trees and potted plants there.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: What about facilities in the cottage?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: It has standard things like a cooker and fridge, I presume.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: In the kitchen area, there's a fridge freezer.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: And we've just put in an electric cooker.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Is there a washing machine?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, there's also a TV in the living room which plays DVDs too.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: The bathroom is too small for a bath, so there's a shower instead.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: I think a lot of people prefer that nowadays anyway.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It's more environmentally friendly, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Unless you spend half the day in it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: What about heating?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: It sometimes gets quite cool at that time of year.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: There's central heating, and if you want to light a fire, there's a stove.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: We can provide all the wood you need for it.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It smells so much nicer than coal, and it makes the room very cozy.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: We've got one in our own house.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: That sounds very pleasant.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Perhaps we should come in the winter to make the most of it.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, we find we don't want to go out when we've got the fire burning.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: There are some attractive views from the cottage which I haven't mentioned.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: There's a famous stone bridge, it's one of the oldest in the region, and you can see it from the living room.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: It isn't far away.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: The bedroom window looks in the opposite direction, and has a lovely view of the hills, and the Monument at the top.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Well, uh, that all sounds perfect.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: I'd like to book it, please.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Would you want a deposit?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Yes, we ask for 30% to secure your booking.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: So that'll be, um, £144.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: And when would you like the rest of the money?
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: You're coming in May, so the last day of March, please.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: TOM

audioscript.text: Excellent.
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: Could I just take your details so that I can...
audioscript.speaker: SHIRLEY

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Holiday rental

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a town councilor reporting on the local transport network and a recreation facility.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, next on the agenda, we have traffic and highways.
audioscript.speaker: CHAIRPERSON

audioscript.text: Councilor Thornton.
audioscript.speaker: CHAIRPERSON

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: Well, we now have the results of the survey carried out last month.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: About traffic and road transport in the town.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: People were generally satisfied with the state of the roads.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: There were one or two complaints about potholes which will be addressed.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But a significant number of people complained about the increasing number of heavy vehicles using our local roads to avoid traffic elsewhere.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We'd expected more complaints by commuters about the reduction in the train service.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But it doesn't seem to have affected people too much.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: The cycle path that runs alongside the river is very well used by both cyclists and pedestrians, since the surface was improved last year, but overtaking can be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: So we're going to add a bit on the side to make it wider.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: At some stage, we'd like to extend the path, so that it goes all the way through the town, but that won't be happening in the immediate future.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: The plans to have a pedestrian crossing next to the Post Office have unfortunately had to be put on hold for the time being.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We'd budgeted for this to be done this financial year.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But then there were rumors that the Post Office was going to move, which would have meant there wasn't really a need for a crossing.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: Now they've confirmed that they're staying where they are.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But the Highways Department have told us that it would be dangerous to have a pedestrian crossing where we'd originally planned it, as there's a bend in the road there, so that'll need some more thought.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: On Station Road near the station and level crossing, drivers can face quite long waits if the level crossing's closed, and we've now got signs up requesting them not to leave their engines running at that time.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: This means pedestrians waiting on the pavement to cross the railway line don't have to breathe in car fumes.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We've had some problems with cyclists leaving their bikes chained to the railings outside the ticket office.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But the station has agreed to provide bike racks there.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So next on the agenda is 'Proposals for improvements to the recreation ground'. Councilor Thornton again.
audioscript.speaker: CHAIRPERSON

audioscript.text: Well, since we managed to extend the recreation ground, we've spent some time talking to local people.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: About how it could be made a more attractive and useful space.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: If you have a look at the map up on the screen, you can see the river up in the north, and the Community Hall near the entrance from the road.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: At present, cars can park between the Community Hall and that line of trees to the east, but this is quite dangerous for pedestrians.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: So we're suggesting a new car park on the opposite side of the Community Hall right next to it.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We also have a new location for the cricket pitch, as we've now purchased additional space to the east of the recreation ground, beyond the trees.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We plan to move it away from its current location, which is rather near the road, into this new area beyond the line of trees.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: This means there's less danger of stray balls hitting cars or pedestrians.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We've got plans for a children's playground, which will be accessible by a footpath from the Community Hall, and will be alongside the river.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We'd originally thought of having it close to the road.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: But we think this will be a more attractive location.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: The skateboard ramp is very popular with both younger and older children.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: We had considered moving this up towards the river, but in the end we decided to have it in the southeast corner near the road.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: The pavilion is very well used at present by both football players and cricketers.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: It will stay where it is now, to the left of the line of trees and near to the river.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: Handy for both the football and cricket pitches, and finally, we'll be getting a new notice board for local information, and that will be directly on people's right as they go from the road into the recreation ground.
audioscript.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: But the station has agreed to provide bike racks there.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: So next on the agenda is 'Proposals for improvements to the recreation ground'. Councilor Thornton again.
utterances.speaker: CHAIRPERSON

utterances.text: Well, since we managed to extend the recreation ground, we've spent some time talking to local people.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: About how it could be made a more attractive and useful space.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: If you have a look at the map up on the screen, you can see the river up in the north, and the Community Hall near the entrance from the road.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: At present, cars can park between the Community Hall and that line of trees to the east, but this is quite dangerous for pedestrians.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: So we're suggesting a new car park on the opposite side of the Community Hall right next to it.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: We also have a new location for the cricket pitch, as we've now purchased additional space to the east of the recreation ground, beyond the trees.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: We plan to move it away from its current location, which is rather near the road, into this new area beyond the line of trees.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: This means there's less danger of stray balls hitting cars or pedestrians.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: We've got plans for a children's playground, which will be accessible by a footpath from the Community Hall, and will be alongside the river.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: We'd originally thought of having it close to the road.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: But we think this will be a more attractive location.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: The skateboard ramp is very popular with both younger and older children.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: We had considered moving this up towards the river, but in the end we decided to have it in the southeast corner near the road.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: The pavilion is very well used at present by both football players and cricketers.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: It will stay where it is now, to the left of the line of trees and near to the river.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: Handy for both the football and cricket pitches, and finally, we'll be getting a new notice board for local information, and that will be directly on people's right as they go from the road into the recreation ground.
utterances.speaker: COUNCILLOR THORNTON

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: A survey found people's main concern about traffic in the area was
options: changes in the type of traffic.

items.title: Which change will shortly be made to the cycle path next to the river?
Plans for a pedestrian crossing have been postponed because
options: funding is not available at present.

items.title: On Station Road, notices have been erected
options: telling cyclists not to leave their bikes outside the station ticket office.
asking motorists to switch off engines when waiting at the level crossing.
warning pedestrians to leave enough time when crossing the railway line.

body.title: Local council report on traffic and highways

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two urban planning students discussing bike-sharing schemes in different cities.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now that we've done all the research into bike-sharing schemes in cities around the world, we need to think about how we're going to organize our report.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: I think we should start by talking about the benefits.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: I mean it's great that so many cities have introduced these schemes where anyone can pick up a bike from dozens of different locations and hire it for a few hours.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: It makes riding a bike very convenient for people.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Yes, but the costs can add up, and that puts people on low incomes off in some places.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Hmm, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: But if it means more people in general are cycling rather than driving, then because they're increasing the amount of physical activity they do, it's good for their health.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But isn't that of less importance?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I mean, doesn't the impact of reduced emissions on air pollution have a more significant effect on people's health?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Certainly in some cities, bike-sharing has made a big contribution to that, and also helped to cut the number of cars on the road significantly.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Which is the main point.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: But I'd say it's had less of an impact on noise pollution, because there are still loads of buses and lorries around.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Shall we quickly discuss the recommendations we're going to make?
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: In order to ensure bike-sharing schemes are successful?
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Well, while I think it's nice to have really state-of-the-art bikes with things like GPS,
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I wouldn't say they're absolutely necessary.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But some technical things are really important, like a fully functional app, so people can make payments and book bikes easily.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Places which haven't invested in that have really struggled.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Good point.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Some people say there shouldn't be competing companies offering separate bike-sharing schemes.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But in some really big cities, competition's beneficial.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: And anyway, one company might not be able to manage the whole thing.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Deciding how much to invest is a big question.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Cities which have opened loads of new bike lanes at the same time as introducing bike-sharing schemes have generally been more successful, but there are examples of successful schemes where this hasn't happened.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: What does matter though, is having a big publicity campaign.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Definitely.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: If people don't know how to use the scheme, or don't understand its benefits, they won't use it.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: People need a lot of persuasion to stop using their cars.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Shall we look at some examples now, and say what we think is good or bad about them?
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: I suppose we should start with Amsterdam, as this was one of the first cities to have a bike-sharing scheme.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: There was already a strong culture of cycling here.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: In a way, it's strange that there was such a demand for bike-sharing, because you'd have thought most people would have used their own bikes.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: And yet it's one of the best used schemes.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Dublin's an interesting example of a success story.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It must be because the public transport system's quite limited.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Hmm. Not really.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: There's no underground, but there are trams and a good bus network.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I'd say price has a lot to do with it.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It's one of the cheapest schemes in Europe to join.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: But the buses are really slow.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Anyway, the weather certainly can't be a factor.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: No, definitely not.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: The London scheme's been quite successful.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's been a really good thing for the city.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: The bikes are popular, and the whole system is well maintained, but it isn't expanding quickly enough.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Basically not enough's been spent on increasing the number of cycle lanes, hopefully that'll change.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Yes, now what about outside Europe?
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Well, bike-sharing schemes have taken off in places like Buenos Aires.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Hmm, they built a huge network of cycle lanes to support the introduction of the scheme there, didn't they?
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: It attracted huge numbers of cyclists where previously there were hardly any.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: An example of good planning.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: New York is a good example of how not to introduce a scheme.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: When they launched it, it was more than 10 times the price of most other schemes.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: More than it costs to take a taxi, crazy.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I think the organizers lacked vision and ambition there.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I think so too.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Sydney would be a good example to use.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: I would have expected it to have grown pretty quickly here.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: Yes, I can't quite work out why it hasn't been an instant success like some of the others.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: It's a shame really.
audioscript.speaker: JAKE

audioscript.text: I know.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: OK, so now we've thought about all those.
audioscript.speaker: AMY

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO benefits of city bike-sharing schemes do the students agree are the most important?
Which TWO things do the students think are necessary for successful bike-sharing schemes?
options: Bikes should have a GPS system.
The app should be easy to use.
Only one scheme should be available.
There should be a large network of cycle lanes.

text: What is the speakers' opinion of the bike-sharing schemes in each of the following cities?
textReadable: What is the speakers' opinion of the bike-sharing schemes in each of the following cities?

list: They agree it has been disappointing.
They think it should be cheaper.
They are surprised it has been so successful.
They agree that more investment is required.
They think the system has been well designed.
They disagree about the reasons for its success.
They think it has expanded too quickly.
body.title: Cities

test_text.title: The Dodo: From History to Extinction

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about a large bird called the dodo, which is now extinct.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: One of the most famous cases of extinction is that of a bird known as the dodo.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, there's even a saying in English, as dead as the dodo.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Used to refer to something which no longer exists.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But for many centuries, the dodo was alive and well, although it could only be found in one place, the island of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was a very large bird, about 1 meter tall, and over the centuries it had lost the ability to fly, but it survived happily under the trees that covered the island.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, in the year 1507, the first Portuguese ships stopped at the island.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The sailors were carrying spices back to Europe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And found the island a convenient stopping place where they could stock up with food and water for the rest of the voyage, but they didn't settle on Mauritius.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, in 1638 the Dutch arrived and set up a colony there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These first human inhabitants of the island found the dodo birds a convenient source of meat, although not everyone liked the taste.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's hard to get an accurate description of what the dodo actually looked like.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We do have some written records from sailors, and a few pictures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we don't know how reliable these are.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The best known picture is a Dutch painting, in which the bird appears to be extremely fat, but this may not be accurate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: An Indian painting done at the same time shows a much thinner bird.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although attempts were made to preserve the bodies of some of the birds, no complete specimen survives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the early 17th century, four dried parts of a bird were known to exist.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of these, three have disappeared.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So only one example of soft tissue from the dodo survives, a dodo head.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Bones have also been found, but there's only one complete skeleton in existence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This single dodo skeleton has recently been the subject of scientific research, which suggests that many of the earlier beliefs about dodos may have been incorrect.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, early accounts of the birds mention how slow and clumsy it was, but scientists now believe the bird's strong knee joints would have made it capable of movement, which was not slow, but actually quite fast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, one 17th century sailor wrote that he found the birds hard to catch.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's true that the dodo's small wings wouldn't have allowed it to leave the ground, but the scientists suggest that these were probably employed for balance,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: while going over uneven ground. Another group of scientists carried out analysis of the dodo's skull.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They found that the reports of the lack of intelligence of the dodo were not borne out by their research, which suggested the bird's brain was not small.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But average in size.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, in relation to its body size, it was similar to that of the pigeon, which is known to be a highly intelligent bird.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The researchers also found that the structure of the bird's skull suggested that one sense which was particularly well developed was that of smell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, the dodo may also have been particularly good at locating ripe fruit and other food in the island's thick vegetation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So it looks as if the dodo was better able to survive and defend itself than was originally believed, yet less than 200 years after Europeans first arrived on the island, they had become extinct.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what was the reason for this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For a long time, it was believed that the dodos were hunted to extinction, but scientists now believe the situation was more complicated than this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another factor may have been the new species brought to the island by the sailors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These included dogs, which would have been a threat to the dodos, and also monkeys, which ate the fruit that was the main part of the dodo's diet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These were brought to the island deliberately.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ships also brought another type of creature, rats, which came to land from the ships and rapidly overran the island.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These upset the ecology of the island, not just the dodos, but other species too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, they were a particular danger to the dodos, because they consumed their eggs, and since each dodo only laid one at a time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This probably had a devastating effect on populations.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, we now think that probably the main cause of the bird's extinction was not the introduction of non-native species.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the introduction of agriculture.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This meant that the forest that had once covered all the island, and that had provided a perfect home for the dodo, was cut down.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So that crops such as sugar could be grown.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, although the dodo had survived for thousands of years, suddenly it was gone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear part of an environmental science lecture about a large bird called the dodo, which is now extinct.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: One of the most famous cases of extinction is that of a bird known as the dodo.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, there's even a saying in English, as dead as the dodo.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Used to refer to something which no longer exists.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But for many centuries, the dodo was alive and well, although it could only be found in one place, the island of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was a very large bird, about 1 meter tall, and over the centuries it had lost the ability to fly, but it survived happily under the trees that covered the island.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, in the year 1507, the first Portuguese ships stopped at the island.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The sailors were carrying spices back to Europe.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And found the island a convenient stopping place where they could stock up with food and water for the rest of the voyage, but they didn't settle on Mauritius.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, in 1638 the Dutch arrived and set up a colony there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These first human inhabitants of the island found the dodo birds a convenient source of meat, although not everyone liked the taste.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's hard to get an accurate description of what the dodo actually looked like.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We do have some written records from sailors, and a few pictures.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we don't know how reliable these are.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The best known picture is a Dutch painting, in which the bird appears to be extremely fat, but this may not be accurate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: An Indian painting done at the same time shows a much thinner bird.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Although attempts were made to preserve the bodies of some of the birds, no complete specimen survives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the early 17th century, four dried parts of a bird were known to exist.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of these, three have disappeared.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So only one example of soft tissue from the dodo survives, a dodo head.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Bones have also been found, but there's only one complete skeleton in existence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This single dodo skeleton has recently been the subject of scientific research, which suggests that many of the earlier beliefs about dodos may have been incorrect.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, early accounts of the birds mention how slow and clumsy it was, but scientists now believe the bird's strong knee joints would have made it capable of movement, which was not slow, but actually quite fast.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, one 17th century sailor wrote that he found the birds hard to catch.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's true that the dodo's small wings wouldn't have allowed it to leave the ground, but the scientists suggest that these were probably employed for balance,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: while going over uneven ground. Another group of scientists carried out analysis of the dodo's skull.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They found that the reports of the lack of intelligence of the dodo were not borne out by their research, which suggested the bird's brain was not small.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But average in size.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, in relation to its body size, it was similar to that of the pigeon, which is known to be a highly intelligent bird.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The researchers also found that the structure of the bird's skull suggested that one sense which was particularly well developed was that of smell.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, the dodo may also have been particularly good at locating ripe fruit and other food in the island's thick vegetation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So it looks as if the dodo was better able to survive and defend itself than was originally believed, yet less than 200 years after Europeans first arrived on the island, they had become extinct.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what was the reason for this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For a long time, it was believed that the dodos were hunted to extinction, but scientists now believe the situation was more complicated than this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another factor may have been the new species brought to the island by the sailors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These included dogs, which would have been a threat to the dodos, and also monkeys, which ate the fruit that was the main part of the dodo's diet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These were brought to the island deliberately.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ships also brought another type of creature, rats, which came to land from the ships and rapidly overran the island.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These upset the ecology of the island, not just the dodos, but other species too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, they were a particular danger to the dodos, because they consumed their eggs, and since each dodo only laid one at a time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This probably had a devastating effect on populations.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, we now think that probably the main cause of the bird's extinction was not the introduction of non-native species.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the introduction of agriculture.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This meant that the forest that had once covered all the island, and that had provided a perfect home for the dodo, was cut down.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So that crops such as sugar could be grown.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, although the dodo had survived for thousands of years, suddenly it was gone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: The dodo was a large flightless bird which used to inhabit the island of Mauritius.
1507 - Portuguese ships transporting <input> stopped at the island to collect food and water.
1638 - The Dutch established a <input> on the island.
They killed the dodo birds for their meat.
The last one was killed in 1681.
items.title: History

items: The only record we have is written descriptions and pictures (possibly unreliable).
A Dutch painting suggests the dodo was very <input>.
The only remaining soft tissue is a dried <input>.
Recent studies of a dodo skeleton suggest the birds were capable of rapid <input>.
It's thought they were able to use their small wings to maintain <input>.
Their sense of <input> enabled them to find food.
items.title: Description

items: Hunting was probably not the main cause.
<input> also escaped onto the island and ate the birds' eggs.
The arrival of farming meant the <input> was destroyed.
items.title: Reasons for extinction

body.title: THE EXTINCTION OF THE DODO BIRD
